frameworks.name: drift-diffusion model
frameworks.one_line: Accumulator with collapsing boundaries cast as optimal value-based decision policy.

frameworks.name: normative / optimal decision theory
frameworks.one_line: Derives the reward-maximising stopping rule given prior over reward contingencies.

frameworks.name: perceptual decision-making (signal accumulation)
frameworks.one_line: Long-standing analogue whose normative status is here extended to value.

frameworks.name: expected-utility theory (non-linear utility)
frameworks.one_line: Relaxing linear-utility assumption breaks the optimality of diffusion.

core_question: Why do drift-diffusion-like dynamics describe value-based decisions, and under what assumptions are they actually optimal?
key_assumptions: agent has a priori knowledge of reward contingencies
evidence about value accumulates as a diffusion process
decision is made when accumulated evidence crosses a boundary
manipulations: task assumptions varied (linear vs non-linear utility)
shape of decision boundary (constant vs time-collapsing)
key_findings: diffusion models implement the optimal value-based decision strategy under stated assumptions
optimality requires decision boundaries that collapse over time
optimal boundaries depend on prior over reward contingencies
diffusion ceases to be optimal once linear-utility assumption is relaxed
interpretations: shared diffusion-like dynamics across perceptual and value-based decisions reflect a common normative principle, not coincidence
framework predicts specific regimes (e.g. non-linear utility) where value and perceptual decisions should diverge
limitations_noted: optimality holds only under specific task assumptions
non-linear utility functions undermine the optimal-diffusion result
